coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn on detection of bilirubin using Fe (III) modulated BSA stabilized copper nanocluster; A mechanistic perception
coredata.dc:creator: Jayasree, M.
Aparna, R.S.
Anjana, R.R.
Anjali Devi, J.S.
John, Nebu
Abha, K.
Manikandan, A.
George, Sony
coredata.dc:description: Hyperbilirubinemia is the condition when bilirubin exceeds normal concentration in body (19.80 mg/mL in newborns and 1.19 × 10−2 mg/mL in adults). Bilirubin encephalopathy in newborns may cause irreversible neurological disorders. Current methods for detection of bilirubin suffer from compromising accuracy. In the present work, bovine serum albumin stabilized copper nanocluster (BSA-CuNCs) was synthesized via a one pot microwave assisted method as a turn on detector for bilirubin. The synthesized BSA-CuNCs having size less than 4 nm, exhibited bright blue emission at 405 nm. Interestingly, no observable change in fluorescence emission was noticed over a wide pH range (1–11) or at high ionic conditions. However, the interaction of Fe3+ with BSA-CuNCs induces quenching of fluorescence. Moreover, the fluorescence can be regained by the addition of bilirubin over other possible coexisting biomolecules. A good linearity was observed for BSA-CuNCs based turn on probe with a Limit of Detection (LoD) 6.62 nM. Furthermore, real sample analyses were carried out with human serum and urine which showed good recovery percentage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bilirubin
BSA
CuNCs
Fluorescence
Turn on sensor
Bilirubin encephalopathy
Quenching

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: The Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge Professor and Head of the Department of Chemistry, University of Kerala, for providing research facilities and SAIF, Kerala, for providing TCSPC and TEM characterization facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-Cu Nanocluster at different pH (λex = 320 nm); (b) The effect of varying ionic strength on the fluorescence of BSA-Cu Nanocluster (Concentration of KCl used are 66 μM, 96 μM, 322 μM, 445 μM, 566 μM, 625 μM respectively at pH = 11.4, λex = 320 nm); (c) & (d) Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) image of BSA-Cu Nanocluster Inset; The Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) Pattern of BSA-CuNCs; (e) Excitation and Emission spectra of BSA-Cu Nanocluster (λex = 320 nm, λem = 405 nm); (f) Fluorescence spectra showing the excitation dependent emission of BSA-Cu Nanocluster synthesized at pH 11.4.

(A) Fluorescence response of BSA-Cu Nanocluster revealing the quenching effect of Fe3+ observed at pH = 11.4; (b) Fluorescence intensity of BSA-Cu nanocluster at various pH with the addition of 66 μM of Fe3+ (λex = 320 nm); (c) Stern-Volmer plot revealing the quenching effect of Fe3+ ions on BSA-Cu Nanocluster at pH 11.4, (λex = 320 nm); (d) The selectivity of BSA-Cu Nanocluster towards Fe3+ ion (pH 11.4; Concentration of each metal ion was taken as 66 μM; λex = 320 nm) were I = Intensity of the BSA-Cu Nanocluster and I0 = Intensity of the probe (BSA-CuNCs/Fe3+).

(A) Fluorescence response of Fe3+ quenched BSA-Cu Nanocluster upon the addition of bilirubin (pH = 11.4; λex = 320 nm, [Fe3+] = 66 μM, [Bilirubin] = 0.01–1.33 μM); (b) Fluorescence response showing the turn on intensity of bilirubin on Fe3+ quenched BSA-Cu Nanocluster at various pH (pH = 2.5, 4.8, 6.5,7.4 and 11.4; λex = 320 nm, [Fe3+] = 66 μM, [Bilirubin] = 10 μM); (c) Linear plot of Fe3+ quenched BSA-Cu Nanocluster showing fluorescence enhancement upon the addition of bilirubin, I = Fluorescence intensity of the turn on system, Iq = Fluorescence intensity of the quenched system; (d) Fluorescence decay profile of BSA-Cu Nanocluster (red line), Fe3+ quenched BSA-CuNCs (blue line) and bilirubin added Fe3+ quenched BSA-Cu Nanocluster (green line) (λex of laser source = 330 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The interference study of Fe3+ quenched BSA-Cu nanocluster using 10 μM concentration each of various biomolecules (pH = 11.4, λex = 320 nm); (b) Fluorescence turn on response of the probe (BSA-CuNCs/Fe3+) with increasing concentration of bilirubin under the cumulative effect of coexisting biomolecules at pH 11.4. (Iq = Intensity of quenched system, I = intensity of the fluorescence turn on system, Coexisting biomolecules: 10 μM each of creatine, galactose, glucose, fructose, urea, uric acid, glutathione, cholesterol and hemoglobin, λex = 320 nm, [Bilirubin] = 0–0.864 μM). (c) Photographs of probe in presence of various coexisting biomolecules during day light and UV light (365 nm).
Scheme depicting microwave assisted synthesis of BSA-CuNCs further Turn off with Fe3+ and Turn on with Bilirubin.
The comparison of performance of the developed probe in the current work with previous reports.

row.entry: Fluorescence enhancement of copper ion quenched gold nanocluster.
Fluorescence quenching of HSA protected gold nanocluters.
Peroxidase like catalytic oxidation of HSA protected gold nanoclusters.
Covalently immobilized HSA protected gold nanocluters over indium tin oxide.
Bilirubin interference in uricase-peroxidase coupled reaction.
Diazotisation- 2,4 dichloroaniline diazotized and its coupling with bilirubin.
Fluorescence enhancement of Fe3+ quenched BSA-Cu Nanoclusters.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn on detection of bilirubin using Fe (III) modulated BSA stabilized copper nanocluster; A mechanistic perception

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, University of Kerala, Kariavattom Campus, Thiruvananthapuram 695581, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hyperbilirubinemia is the condition when bilirubin exceeds normal concentration in body (19.80 mg/mL in newborns and 1.19 × 10−2 mg/mL in adults). Bilirubin encephalopathy in newborns may cause irreversible neurological disorders. Current methods for detection of bilirubin suffer from compromising accuracy. In the present work, bovine serum albumin stabilized copper nanocluster (BSA-CuNCs) was synthesized via a one pot microwave assisted method as a turn on detector for bilirubin. The synthesized BSA-CuNCs having size less than 4 nm, exhibited bright blue emission at 405 nm. Interestingly, no observable change in fluorescence emission was noticed over a wide pH range (1–11) or at high ionic conditions. However, the interaction of Fe3+ with BSA-CuNCs induces quenching of fluorescence. Moreover, the fluorescence can be regained by the addition of bilirubin over other possible coexisting biomolecules. A good linearity was observed for BSA-CuNCs based turn on probe with a Limit of Detection (LoD) 6.62 nM. Furthermore, real sample analyses were carried out with human serum and urine which showed good recovery percentage.

ce:section.ce:para: Hyperbilirubinemia is an alarming condition in which the accumulation of bilirubin in blood exceeds the normal concentration (34–43 μM) [1]. Bilirubin is a yellow orange pigment formed from the heme catabolism [2]. Newborns are not easily able to get rid off bilirubin and it can build up in their body. Furthermore, excess bilirubin causes yellowing of baby's skin, eyes and other tissues resulting in a condition called jaundice [3]. In addition, bilirubin encephalopathy in newborns will cause permanent impairment of nervous function [1]. Under normal physiological condition, bilirubin secreted in bile is conjugated and eliminated from the body [1]. Thus, free bilirubin concentration in body fluids is a parameter to detect hyperbilirubinemia [3]. Current established methods of bilirubin detection include, diazo method and enzyme based detection methods, but these methods generally suffer from compromising accuracy [4–11]. In spectrophotometric detection, non-bilirubin yellow pigment can act as a source of error [12]. Furthermore, enzymatic methods suffer from high cost, low stability and they depend on reaction conditions [3]. Recently, a fairly inexpensive method to detect bilirubin using phototherapy treatment was established [13].

Fluorescent methods are simple, sensitive and concentration up to trace amounts can be detected [14]. To date, fluorescent methods based on turn off and turn on sensors has been reported in the detection of bilirubin. But turn on sensors are more preferable than turn off sensors because, it is easy to observe a bright signal in a dark background rather than a bright signal at bright background. In addition to this, turn on is more selective than turn off since the quenched fluorescence is only restored by interaction with preferred analyte [15]. Current fluorescence applications mostly involve organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent proteins [16]. However, the use of quantum dots and organic dyes in fluorescence detection was limited by their photo bleaching and toxic nature [16]. Metal nanoclusters have been developed as a new class of fluorophore which exhibit improved photo physical properties, color tunability and high surface to volume ratios [17].

Metal nanoclusters consist of a few to hundred atoms and their sizes are comparable to the Fermi wavelength of electrons which makes them a missing link between metal atoms and metal nanoparticles [18]. In addition to this, they exhibit discrete energy levels and molecule like properties including HOMO-LUMO transitions and luminescence [19]. To date, studies have been focused on the synthesis of gold and silver while studies on copper nanocluster (CuNCs) are in infancy. Compared to noble metal clusters, copper is significantly cheaper and has optical properties similar to silver and gold nanoclusters [20]. Furthermore, the precursors of CuNCs are cheaper and relatively abundant when compared to AuNCs and AgNCs [20]. In addition, incorporating CuNCs with large ligand like Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) will make them more biocompatible and stable to chemical environment [21]. Thus, current study mainly focuses on CuNCs coated with BSA.

Very recently, studies have been reported on gold nanoclusters quenched by Cu2+ as fluorescent probe for the detection of bilirubin [3,22]. In this perceptive, we explored BSA stabilized CuNCs quenched with Fe3+ as fluorescence Turn On detection for bilirubin. The sensitivity and selectivity of the developed probe was evaluated with different probable co-existing biomolecules. The performance was assessed in real samples also. The fabricated CuNCs based Turn On sensor can be developed for a commercially viable point of care detection method adaptable in primary health care centres.

CuSO4·5H2O, Quinine Sulphate, Iron (III) Chloride hexahydrate, Iron (II) Chloride hexahydrate was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. NaOH pellets, Glucose, Galactose, conc. HCl and Uric acid were acquired from Alfa Aesar. Potassium chloride, Calcium nitrate, 30% H2O2 was obtained from Nice Chemicals Pvt Ltd., Cochin, India. Fructose, Cholesterol, Urea and Creatine were bought from SRL, India. Glutathione, Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) and Magnesium Sulphate purchased from Merck, India., Haemoglobin and Bilirubin were purchased from TCI, Tokyo, Japan. All other metal ions were purchased from Spectrum Chemicals. All reagents were used without further purification. Millipore water is used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on Jasco FP-750 Spectrofluorometer. Perkin Elmer Lambda 365 UV-vis spectrophotometer was used to study the absorption spectra of the samples. Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopic (ATR-FTIR) data was acquired using Aligent Technology Cary 360 FTIR spectrometer over the range 400–4000 cm−1. The size and morphology of the synthesized sample was analyzed using JEOL JEM 2100 Transmission Electron Microscopy in 200 kV. Digital pH meter MK VI was used to measure pH at room temperature. Lifetime measurements were carried out using Flurolog Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Horiba, USA, in TCSPC mode. The samples were prepared by micro wave irradiation using Power Solo 17D, Onida Instrument.

Briefly, CuSO4 solution (5 mL, 20 mM) was mixed with BSA solution (0.200 mg in 20 mL) under vigorous stirring for 5 min. The pH was adjusted to 12 by 0.1 M NaOH solution. Allow to stir for few more minutes. Then 15 mL 30% H2O2 was added with stirring for 5 min, then irradiate in microwave for 40 s at 280 W. The brown colored solution obtained after microwave irradiation showed no immediate fluorescence. On standing for 24 h, the color changes to light violet and stored in dark at 4° C for further use.

The quantum yield of BSA-CuNCs was measured in reference to quinine sulphate solution (in 0.1 M H2SO4). The quantum yield is calculated using the equation,
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
were, ‘ƞ’ is the refractive index, ‘I’ and ‘A’ are emission intensity and absorption respectively. The subscript ‘R’ stands for reference (ϕ
R = 0.54 and ƞ = 1.33).

Fluorescence response of BSA-CuNCs towards Fe3+ was monitored by incremental addition of 20 μL of 10 mM Fe3+ ion solution. Fluorescence intensity was monitored at emission on 405 nm by applying an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, immediately after the addition. The selectivity of BSA-CuNCs to Fe3+ ions over other metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+and Hg2+) and anions (CO3 2−, PO4 3−, Cl−, SO4 2− and CH3COO−) were investigated through the same procedure.

The typical procedure for the detection of bilirubin is as follows. 3 mL of the CuNCs was half quenched with 20 μL of 10 mM Fe3+ (66 μM) at pH 11.4. Then, equivalents of bilirubin were added stepwise. Fluorescence measurements were taken immediately after the addition of bilirubin. Similar procedure was followed to investigate the interference of probe towards bilirubin over other coexisting biomolecules (creatine, galactose, glucose, fructose, urea, uric acid, glutathione (GSH), cholesterol and haemoglobin). Control experiments were carried out with water to nullify the effect caused by the dilution. All the experiments were done in triplicate and batch to batch reproducibility was confirmed.

Human serum samples and urine samples were collected in the morning from healthy volunteers. Suitable concentration of bilirubin was spiked (16 μM) for analysis. The samples were diluted to 10, 100 and 1000 times. Furthermore, individual interference study mimicking urine samples of patients with lifestyle disease conditions (diabetes, chronic kidney failure and uremic conditions) was investigated by spiking higher concentration of glucose, creatine and urea in urine samples. The analysis was conducted without any pre-treatment at different dilutions.

The current study involves the preparation of bright blue fluorescent Cu nanocluster using BSA as a stabilizer, via microwave assisted synthesis. BSA contains a large number of cysteine and tyrosine residues which has potential binding affinity to metal ions [23–25]. Moreover, the functional groups in BSA such as -COOH, -NH and -SH provides a suitable platform for the interaction of copper ions [23]. The sensing mechanism is diagrammatically represented in schematics 1 . Furthermore, BSA can withstand wide range of pH and can be easily conjugated with other systems which enable further biological applicability [23]. Herein, BSA solution and CuSO4 are mixed to form a blue colored viscous paste. To increase the solubility of mixture, the pH was modulated and the maximum solubility was observed at pH 11.4. At this pH, the blue colored solution turns to purple colored BSA-Cu complex [Fig. S1]. In contrast to the earlier methods reported [5] current study involves a microwave mediated synthesis. The microwave conditions were optimized and BSA-CuNCs showed maximum intensity at 280 W, 40 s [Fig. S2]. Fluorescence spectra were taken immediately after the synthesis, but no response was observed. Then, incubation for 24 h gave a fluorescence response but with weak fluorescence intensity. Thus, BSA was not an ideal reducing agent and H2O2 was added as an additive. Different volumes of 30% H2O2 were tried and the maximum intensity was observed for 15 mL 30% H2O2 (Fig. S3a) and was fixed throughout the synthesis. The addition of 15 mL of H2O2 resulted in a brown colored solution [Fig. S1] and was microwave irradiated. The brown solution obtained after microwave irradiation was incubated for 24 h to obtain a transparent violet solution (Fig. S1), which shows a bright blue fluorescence under UV light illumination (365 nm). The quantum yield was calculated to be 6.4% using quinine sulphate as standard. Here, H2O2 has two roles. It will act as a ligand, which combine with Cu-BSA to form Cu-BSA-H2O2 complex which in turn hasten the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu0 [26]. The disulfide bonds in BSA were partly destroyed by H2O2. This accelerates the formation of CuNCs by the exposure of free amino groups in BSA [5]. The synthesized BSA-CuNCs showed emission peak at 405 nm when excited at 320 nm, whereas its precursors failed to give such characteristic emission, demonstrating the formation of BSA-CuNCs (Fig. S3b). The effect of pH on the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs was studied under wide pH range (1–11.4) and maximum intensity was observed at pH 11.4 (Fig. 1 a). This was attributed by the increase in availability of lone pair of electrons of BSA at basic pH [27]. Thus further experiments were carried out at pH 11.4. Ionic strength has potential influence fluorescence quenching mechanisms [28]. Furthermore, the stability of BSA-CuNCs under varying ionic strength will increase its validity in real sample analysis. The stability in KCl solution was checked and found that the fluorescence intensity of BSA CuNCs shows a marginal shift with the increase in ionic strength (Fig. 1b) and the fluorescence intensity persisted for 45 days without any protection from inert atmosphere when stored in dark at 4 °C (Fig. S3c).

To characterize BSA-CuNCs, Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) and Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopic techniques were employed. To eliminate the unreacted particles, BSA-CuNCs was filtered in a dialysis membrane (12000 Da, 6 h) prior to TEM analysis. The TEM image (Fig. 1 c&d) shows spherical morphology of BSA-CuNCs with average size of 3.3 ± 0.8 nm. When higher resolution was attempted, the protein matrix charred and resolution beyond 2 nm was not possible. The irradiation of stray electron beam caused the nanocluster to fuse into crystalline form [29] and was confirmed by the Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1c inset). The ATR-FTIR spectra of BSA and BSA-CuNCs were taken (Fig. S4). In BSA, the peak near 1540 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 attributes to –NH bending or –CN stretching vibrations and peak near 1635 cm−1 corresponding to amine scissoring. In addition, the peak at 1385 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH bending vibrations in carboxylic acids. Presence of these functionalities favors the formation of BSA-CuNCs. The similar peak is observed in BSA-CuNCs illustrating CuNCs entrapped within BSA. Other characteristic peaks observed in BSA-CuNCs were for –NH bending at 1598 cm−1 and –CN stretching frequency at 1300 cm−1. Absence of –NH scissoring can be correlated to the involvement of –NH moiety with copper during the linkage and subsequent formation of CuNCs.

Upon excitation at 320 nm, the emission peak spans around 405 nm (Fig. 1e). The fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs was originated from the transitions between filled d band and sp band [30]. In addition to this, interaction of metal to ligand surface is another reason for fluorescence [31]. From the UV-vis spectra (Fig. S5), no apparent plasmonic absorption was observed in the visible region. This confirms the absence of large Cu nanoparticles in system [32].

Interestingly, different emission peak were observed for BSA-CuNCs on applying different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1f). This was in violation with Kasha-Vavilov rule of excitation independent emission [33]. This excitation dependent nature was probably due to the non-uniform size distribution of the developed CuNCs observed in TEM characterization. The Cu nanocluster contains ensemble of quantized energy states. Each excitation wavelength corresponds to the emission from particular energy levels [34]. Maximum fluorescence intensity was observed for excitation at 320 nm and was fixed for further studies. With increasing wavelength we observed a decrease in fluorescence intensity with concomitant red shift.

Fluorescence quenching and recovery plays an important role in analytical detection. Interestingly, the blue fluorescence of the BSA-CuNCs can be effectively quenched by the addition of Fe3+ ion. As observed in Fig. 2 a, the addition of increasing concentration of Fe3+ (6–166 μM) had no effect on the emission wavelength of BSA-CuNCs. For better performance, experimental conditions such as time and pH were optimized. As described in Fig. 2b, the quenching effect of Fe3+ was low at acidic conditions. The quenching became noticeable at alkaline pH and optimum fluorescence quenching was observed at pH 11.4. This effect may be attributed by increase in availability of lone pair of electrons of -NH functionality of BSA at basic pH [27]. The fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs was immediately quenched by the addition of Fe3+ and remained almost constant upto 30 min (Fig. S6).

To predict the linear dependence of (I0-I)/I0 and quencher concentration, a linear plot was drawn with (I0-I)/I0 against concentration (Fig. 2c). The fitted linear data was expressed by, (I0-I)/I0 = 0.009 [Fe3+] + 0.349, were I0 is the intensity of the BSA-CuNCs and I is the intensity of the probe (BSA-CuNCs-Fe3+). Furthermore, the R2 value was 0.9806, indicating linearity in quenching over the range 0–70 μM. The Limit of Detection (LoD) was calculated to be 2.5 μM (From the equation, LoD = 3σ/m, were σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements). Time Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) was employed to explore the quenching mechanism (Table S1). BSA-CuNCs and Fe3+-BSA-CuNCs followed triexponential fluorescence decay kinetics. The average fluorescence lifetime of BSA was 6.49 ns whereas for Fe3+-BSA-CuNCs it was 6.14 ns. There was only a negligible difference in average lifetime indicating static quenching.

To investigate the selectivity of BSA-CuNCs towards Fe3+, various metal ions and anions were tested. This include Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Hg+, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, Cl−, SO4 2− and CH3COO− (Fig. S7 a-p). A plot of I0/I against concentration of various metal cations were carried out (Fig. 2d), were I0 is the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs and I is that of quenched system. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, a substantial quenching was observed only for Fe3+ whereas minor effects were offered by Cu2+ and Ag+. But, the possible coexisting anions create no interference on the selectivity of Fe3+. Based on these investigations a plausible mechanism can be outlined.

Generally, there are five mechanisms facilitating quenching of fluorescence such as Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Photo Induced Electron Transfer (PET), Inner Filter Effect (IFE), Electron Transfer and Aggregation Induced Quenching [35]. Quenching follows a FRET mechanism, if there exist an overlap of the emission spectra of BSA-CuNCs and absorption spectra of the quencher (Fe3+) and should followed by a decrease in fluorescence lifetime [35]. On the other hand, excited state electron transfer (PET) prevails if there exists an energy gap between CuNCs and quencher and the quenching should be dynamic [35]. But the fluorescence lifetime studies suggests that the ratio of average lifetimes of BSA-CuNCs (τ0) and quenched system (τ) i.e. τ0/τ ∼1 (1.05) and followed static quenching [36–38]. Moreover, there was no overlap in the emission and absorption spectrum (Fig. S8). Thus, the probability of FRET and PET can be sidelined. There exists a marginal overlap between the excitation spectra of BSA-CuNCs and absorption spectra of Fe3+ (Fig. S8a) leaving the possibility of IFE. But, IFE is an inevitable evil and associated with fluorescence measurements. It is an effect caused by the attenuation of the excitation beam or absorption of radiation emitted by excess concentration of the BSA-CuNCs or the quencher (Fe3+) [39].

Herein, three possible mechanisms play a pivotal role in fluorescence quenching of BSA-CuNCs. Foremost among them is the electron transfer mechanism. This can be correlated with the electron accepting ability of Fe3+ ion. Since Fe3+ possess half-filled d orbital (3 d5 4s0), it provides high acidic nature, charge density and increased electron affinity [40]. Upon addition of Fe3+ ions, complex will be formed between electron deficient Fe3+ and electron rich system (BSA-CuNCs) and weaken the structural compactness leading to static quenching [40]. Among the various metal ions tested, Fe3+ has high charge density and electron withdrawing ability. On the other hand, Mn2+ and Cr3+ with same electronic configuration failed to quench the fluorescence (Fig. S7 e & f) because of the relatively low charge density and standard electrode potential compared to Fe3+ [41]. Besides Fe3+, other metal ions like Cu2+ and Ag+ impart minor effect on the fluorescence quenching (Fig. S7 h, k & m).

Furthermore, there exists another phenomenon in which fluorescence quenching is induced by Fe3+ via aggregation of nanocluster through analyte ligand binding [42]. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) techniques were employed to study the existence of this mechanism. The results (Fig. S8 b-d) indicates that the hydrodynamic diameter of the BSA-CuNCs was found to be 6.8 ± 0.3 nm and that of the quenched species upon the addition of 66 μM Fe3+ was 1098.6 ± 1289.4 nm. Such a high size was attributed by the aggregation of CuNCs in presence of Fe3+ ions.

On the other hand, Fe3+ acts as a universal quencher (quantum dots, carbon dots and metal nanoclusters) [30,43,44]. Based on this inference, a plausible mechanism based on the ferromagnetic effect of Fe3+ can be suggested. According to this mechanism, the discrete energy levels of CuNCs splits further in presence of Fe3+ leading to overlap of energy levels involved in fluorescence phenomena. This caused the relaxation of the electrons to the ground state non-radiatively [45] resulting in decreasing fluorescence intensity. However, further research is needed to explore this mechanism in detail. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence can be restored by interaction with preferred analyte i.e. bilirubin.

Bilirubin has a natural antioxidant property [46] and this can be utilized for the restoration of fluorescence in BSA-CuNCs. BSA-CuNCs was half quenched with an optimum concentration of 66 μM Fe3+ (BSA-CuNCs/Fe3+) was used as the probe for further investigations. Addition of 1 μM bilirubin to the probe (Fe3+ quenched BSA-CuNCs) resulted in the restoration of fluorescence by 80% (Fig. 3 . 1a). pH was modulated to obtain better results (Fig. 3b). Interestingly, fluorescence turn on was maximum at pH 11.4. This was attributed by the increased solubility of bilirubin at alkaline pH [47]. The low sensitivity of the probe (Fe3+ quenched BSA-CuNCs) in pH 7.4 can be correlated with the electron donating ability of –NH2 functionality of BSA at neutral pH. Plot of log [Bilirubin] vs (I-Iq)/Iq was plotted (Fig. S9a). The results showed that turn on phenomena was visible over wide concentration range (0.1 μM–0.1 pM). Using the linear plot drawn, (Fig. 3c) the limit of detection was calculated to be 6.62 nM. Various methods reported for bilirubin detection is tabulated in Table 1 . The addition of bilirubin resulted in the restoration of average fluorescence lifetime of Fe3+/BSA-CuNCs (τavg = 6.14 ns) to that of virgin. BSA-CuNCs (τavg = 6.59 ns) (Table S1). Furthermore, results from the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) studies indicates that upon addition of bilirubin, the disintegration of the Fe3+ induced aggregates leads to fluorescence restoration. In addition to this, time dependent fluorescence response of probe towards bilirubin was investigated (Fig. S9b). The results suggest that, the fluorescence response increases with the standing time. Standing in presence of light, bilirubin undergoes a configurational isomerisation that is the Z,Z-isomer get converted into more soluble Z,E-isomer [48] (Fig. S9b).

Instead of Fe3+ ion, Cu2+ and Ag+ were used to quench the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs and turn on response on addition of bilirubin were studied (Fig. S10 a&b). Bilirubin showed exclusive response towards Fe3+ quenched system though Cu2+ and Ag+ quenched system showed a slight Turn On response. But, the Turn On response elicited by Ag+ and Cu2+ quenched system is not adequate enough to adopt clinical investigation. The exclusive response towards Fe3+ quenched system can be explained by the antioxidant property of bilirubin [46], Redox reactions will take place which convert Fe3+ ion to Fe2+ ion. The charge density and electron affinity of Fe2+ is relatively low when compared to Fe3+. From Fig. 2d, it is evident that quenching is more pronounced for Fe3+ than Fe2+. Moreover, bilirubin possess open tetra pyrrole ring like structure will sequester quenching Fe3+ ion. In addition, affinity between bilirubin and BSA established will be an added advantage in bringing the analyte molecule towards the probe [49]. The overall process is illustrated in Scheme 1.

Potential interference by co-existing biomolecules such as, galactose, urea, glucose, creatine, fructose, glutathione, haemoglobin, uric acid and cholesterol was investigated. Concentrations of all the biomolecules were kept at 10 μM and Fe3+ to be 66 μM. As evident from Fig. 4 a, the coexisting biomolecules such as urea, creatine, glucose, uric acid and glutathione exhibited slightly challenging interference signal, but the turn on intensity of bilirubin remained unperturbed in presence of these biological molecules. In real life situation cumulative effect persists rather than individual effect. To evaluate this, real life scenario was mimicked. 10 μM of all the biomolecules were added to the probe followed by the sequential addition of bilirubin. The result suggests that, the individual interference if any was nullified by the cumulative effect (Fig. 4b) of coexisting biomolecules. Photographs of probe in presence of various coexisting biomolecules during day light and UV light (Fig. 4c) were taken, only bilirubin showed restoration of fluorescence.

In order to evaluate the response behavior of the probe in real situation, tests were carried out in urine and serum. Spike recovery analysis is carried out to detect bilirubin in blood serum and urine samples. Spike recovery is used to test whether the response of a sample to a particular analyte is the same as that expected from a calibration curve [54] in most of the instrument based analysis. Urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers and diluted in logarithmic fashion (10, 100 and 1000 times) and a known amount of bilirubin (16 μM) was spiked. A recovery of 76–98% was obtained (Fig. 5 a, Table S2). In addition to this, interference studies of the probe in case of diabetic, chronic kidney disease and uremic patients were mimicked by suitably spiking known amount of glucose (9.158 mM), creatine (5.109 mM) and urea (0.1548 M) in urine. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, in 100 times dilution the effect of these biomolecules were marginal indicating the specificity of the probe can be increased with dilution due to the increased solubility of bilirubin. Thus the critical challenges exhibited by urea, glucose and creatine get silenced with dilution. Furthermore, cumulative effect was studied by adding higher concentration of urea, creatine and glucose followed by the sequential addition of bilirubin. The result agrees well with previous study carried out in water (Fig. 5c). In addition to urine analysis, studies were also conducted on serum samples. Amount of bilirubin present in adult (170 μM) and newborns (340 μM) vary significantly in serum so that separate analysis was carried out by spiking suitable concentration of bilirubin. The recovery of fluorescence is in the range of 92–98% (Fig. 5d). Further studies are in progress for silencing these interference effect and extension of these results for the development of paper strip sensors.

In summary, we have synthesized CuNCs using BSA via microwave assisted method. The fluorescence of the BSA-CuNCs was selectively quenched by Fe3+ via static quenching mechanism with a limit of detection of 2.5 μM. Addition of bilirubin to the turn off system resulted in the restoration of fluorescence owing to the antioxidant property of bilirubin. The individual interference effect of biomolecules gets nullified at cumulative situations. Furthermore, the practical applicability of the probe was tested in real sample and satisfactory results were obtained at logarithmic dilutions. Performance of the probe was investigated by mimicking real life conditions in case of patients with lifestyle diseases like diabetes, chronic kidney disease and uremic conditions. Interference offered by high concentration of coexisting biomolecules gets silenced at dilution. Insight gain from the present work can be utilized for the future development of sensors for bilirubin.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Professor and Head of the Department of Chemistry, University of Kerala, for providing research facilities and SAIF, Kerala, for providing TCSPC and TEM characterization facilities.
